Title: Comparative analysis of evapotranspiration (ET), crop water stress index (CWSI), and normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) to delineate site-specific irrigation management zones in almond orchards

Data used for MZ: Evapotranspiration (ET); Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI); Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) from aircraft
Auxilary data: Canopy temperature (Tc); Air temperature; Relative humidity; Wind speed
Data used for validation: Almond yield (recorded from 12 locations); Irrigation water applied (measured from 8 different locations)
Sensors/DataSources names: Instrumented aircraft; FLIR A65 thermal sensor; specialized VNIR IDS camera
SamplingDensity: Aerial imagery collected on 11 days in June and July of 2020
Resolution: Original: Thermal and multispectral images 0.6 to 0.7 m; Final Analysis: Rasters resampled to 3 × 3 m pixel size
Methods: Unsupervised k-means clustering algorithm; Surface Energy Balance Algorithm for Land (SEBAL)
Sub-Methods-Internal: Silhouette width (to determine optimum number of zones); Simplified CWSI calculation; Mann-Whitney Wilcoxon test (Wilcox test); Kappa coefficient (for temporal stability); Median filter (for MZ smoothing)
Zones: Two (optimum number determined by silhouette width)
ManagementFocus: Delineate site-specific irrigation management zones; Practice precision irrigation management in woody perennial cropping systems
Validation: Statistical comparison (Mann-Whitney Wilcoxon) of yield and irrigation amounts between zones; Kappa coefficient for spatiotemporal agreement analysis
Notes: This study shows that CWSI can be as effective as ET in delineating irrigation management zones, and both inputs sensitive to capture the vegetation responses to irrigation management during the summer growing season. ET- and CWSI-based management zones addressed higher spatial variability in the field (up to 73.6 %) than NDVI (up to 68 %).

Title: Management Zones Delineation, Correct and Incorrect Application Analysis in a Coriander Field Using Precision Agriculture, Soil Chemical, Granular and Hydraulic Analyses, Fuzzy k-Means Zoning, Factor Analysis and Geostatistics
Country: Greece (Central Greece Region, Thessaly valley, Kileler Municipality, Krannonas municipal unit)
FieldSize: 546.72 m2 (overall cultivated area); Trial plots with a specific area of 0.055 ha
Data used for MZ: Soil chemical, granular, and hydraulic parameters; The full set includes 20 parameters: Phosphorus P-olsen, Potassium K+, Calcium Ca++, Magnesium Mg++, pH, Nitrogen inorganic, Organic matter (OM), Calcium carbonate CaCO3, Clay, Silt, Very fine sand, Sand pr, Gravel, Soil Erodibility [Kfactor], Wilting point ($\theta wp$), Field capacity ($\theta fc$), Saturation ($\theta sat$), Plant available water (PAW), Saturated hydraulic conductivity (Ks), and Bulk density; Maximum extracted Principal Component Analyses (PCAs) were also used for clustering

Resolution: The final analysis involved 144 subplots; Interpolation applied using Ordinary Kriging to obtain twenty parametric GIS maps

Sub-Methods-Internal: Percentage of Management Zones Spatial Agreement (PoMZSA) (percent) calculation; Semivariogram models (Gaussian, Exponential, Stable, Pentaspherical, Tetraspherical, Spherical, Circular); Mahalanobis similarity distance metric (for clustering); Varimax method with Kaiser Normalization (for PCA rotation); Logarithmic transformation (for data normalization of 8 datasets); Unbalanced one-way ANOVA; Cross-validation (LOOCV, MPE, RMSE, MSPE, RMSSE, ASE)
Zones: Four Management Zones (MZs) were finally chosen for application in the trial plots (MZx where x = 1..4); The optimal number of MZs based on fuzzy exploratory analysis for the "soil All parameters group" was k = 4

Notes: The exploratory fuzzy analysis determined the optimal fuzziness exponent as $\phi=1.14$ and the optimal number of MZs as k=4. The FkM Xie and Benny’s index proved robust in pointing clearly to the optimal fuzziness exponent when FPI and MPE values were confusing. When arbitrary fuzziness exponents (e.g., $\phi=1.30$ or $\phi=1.50$) were used for MZ delineation where MZs > 2, the PoMZSA metric revealed low to medium spatial agreement, showing a statistically significant difference between the resulting zones. For clustering, the Mahalanobis similarity distance metric was selected as the best for multivariate clustering based on the field data characteristics. Strong spatial dependence was found in 80.00 percent of the analyzed soil parameters.

Title: Delineating Management Zones with Different Yield Potentials in Soybeanâ€“Corn and Soybeanâ€“Cotton Production Systems
Country: Brazil (Mato Grosso: Sapezal and Querência; Paraná: Arapongas)
FieldSize: Four plots: Plot A (202 ha), Plot B (267 ha), Plot C (156 ha), Plot D (195 ha)
Data used for MZ: Soil attributes (Clay content, Potassium, Apparent Electrical Conductivity (ECa)); Elevation; Historical Yield maps (Cotton Yield, Soybean Yield, Corn Yield); 12 Vegetation Indices (VIs) (EVI, NDVI, RVI, PSRI, GNDVI, TVI, CVI, CIG, CIRE, DVI, NDRE, SAVI), either individually, averaged during peak biomass, consolidated over a time series, or selected by correlation/importance.
Auxilary data: Individual yield maps (standardized to -1, 1 interval for averaging); Sentinel-2 satellite images (for VIs calculation); Linear correlation (R) values between VIs and other attributes.
Data used for validation: Yield Variance Reduction (VR) percentage of the historical yield data (calculated for the whole plot versus MZs); Comparison of Average Normalized Crop Yield values across delineated MZs.
Sensors/DataSources names: Harvest Monitor; Harvest GPS; SoilXplorer Sensor; Sentinel-2 satellite; Google Earth Engine (GEE) platform; R language scripts.
SamplingDensity: Plot A: 1 Clay sample/ha; Plot B: 1 Clay sample/ha; Plot C: 5 Clay/Potassium samples/ha; Plot D: 326 ECa samples; Harvest Monitor/GPS data: 785 to 1265 samples (depending on plot and year); Satellite imagery: 80 to 140 cloud-free images used from 2018 to 2022 (depending on plot).
Resolution: Spatial grid standardized to match the Sentinel-2 satellite grid (10 m/pixel).
Methods: Delineation of Management Zones (MZ); Multivariate analysis; Unsupervised Machine Learning; Clustering; Data Filtering; Data Standardization.
Sub-Methods-Internal: X-means clustering algorithm (hierarchical variant of k-means, estimates optimal number of clusters); Kriging algorithm (for attributes with low sampling density); Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW) (for attributes with high sample density); Linear correlation (R); Random Forest algorithm (for feature selection, using %IncMSE and IncNodePurity); Standardization (-1, 1 interval for yield maps); Histogram analysis (for outlier removal).
Zones: Number of MZs varied by plot and procedure (e.g., Plot A: 2 to 4 MZs; Plot B: 2 to 4 MZs; Plot C: 2 to 3 MZs; Plot D: 2 MZs).
ManagementFocus: Enable spatially differentiated management using precision agriculture tools; Delineate Management Zones (MZs) with varying yield potentials; Improve management of inputs; Provide greater profit to the farmer; Assess the effectiveness of VIs as substitutes for field data in MZ delineation.
Crops: Soybean (summer crop); Corn (winter crop, Plot D); Cotton (second crop/winter, Plots A, B, C).
Validation: Yield Variance Reduction (VR) index; Comparison of average normalized yield between zones
Notes: Four procedures were evaluated for MZ delineation: (I) Field attributes (soil, elevation, yield) + peak-biomass VIs; (II) Soil attributes + well-correlated VIs; (III) VIs replacing all field data via correlation (R); (IV) VIs selected by Random Forest variable importance. For plots with easily identified natural spatial variability (A and B), all procedures generally favored obtaining MZ maps that allowed **reductions of 40% to 70% in yield variance**. In plots with low natural spatial variability and/or unreliable yield maps (Plot C), the different input data sets did not help in obtaining feasible MZ maps, showing minimal yield VR (around 1%). The exclusive use of VIs can effectively replace other types of data, but should be used with reservations in areas where spatially differentiated anthropogenic activities are present or when VIs are consolidated over extended periods in plots with substantial topographical variability (Plot D). The X-means algorithm was chosen for clustering because, unlike k-means, it can estimate the optimal number of clusters based on statistical criteria.

Title: Dynamic delineation of management zones for site-specific nitrogen fertilization in a citrus orchard
FieldSize: Four citrus plots (A: 0.383 ha, 160 trees; B: 0.414 ha, 227 trees; C: 0.515 ha, 292 trees; D: 0.58 ha, 383 trees)
Data used for MZ: Five variables were selected: **Constant (Static)**: Elevation (m), Northness (unitless), and Slope (percent); **Non-constant (Dynamic)**: Canopy N content (percent DW) and Tree height (n-DSM, m)
Auxilary data: Digital Elevation Model (DEM, m); Digital Surface Model (DSM); N content-to-October yield envelope curve; Spectral bands (Blue, Green, Red, Red-Edge, Near-Infrared)
Data used for validation: Measured leaf N content (percent DW) from laboratory analysis; Hand-picked yield assessment (kg) in mid-November 2019 (for envelope curve); Cross-validation of SVM-R model (R2, RMSE, RMSECV); Fuzzy Silhouette Index (FSI); V-measure of spatial association (for comparing cluster maps)
Sensors/DataSources names: Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) platform (T960 TARROT); Multispectral sensor (MicaSense Red-Edge-MX); Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) auto-analyzer (Lachat Instruments); Analog scale (for yield); PIX4Dmapper software; R software (packages: e1071, vegan, spdep, SABRE, sf, fpc)
SamplingDensity: Leaf tissue samples: 12 trees sampled bi-monthly in each of the four plots (total 48 trees per campaign, six dates in total); UAV imaging campaigns: six dates throughout the growing season of 2019; Yield assessment: 48 sampling trees
Resolution: UAV imagery spatial resolution: 5.5 cm per pixel; Final DEM map resolution: 2 m spatial resolution
Methods: Multivariate spatiotemporal dynamic clustering; Fuzzy C-means (FCM) clustering; Support Vector Machine Regression (SVM-R); Hot-spot analysis; Photogrammetry (Structure-from-Motion, SfM)
Sub-Methods-Internal: Getis-Ord Gi* z-score (for standardization and hot-spot analysis); Calinski-Harabasz Index (CHI) (to determine optimal cluster number); Fuzzy Silhouette Index (FSI) (to assess cluster quality); V-measure (to test spatial association between cluster maps); Ordinary Kriging (with a spherical semi-variogram model) (for DEM generation); Normalized Digital Surface Model (n-DSM) calculation; Median absolute deviation (MAD) method (for outlier removal); Histogram matching; Envelope curve approach (for optimal N content)
Zones: Nitrogen (N) Management Zones (MZs); Optimal number of MZs restricted between two and five; Based on constant variables only, plots A and D had two MZs, plot C had three MZs, and plot B had four MZs
ManagementFocus: Develop a multivariate spatiotemporal dynamic clustering approach to generate MZs; Optimize N fertilization/management; Support site-specific and time-specific nitrogen management (SSNM); Minimize environmental pollution and N losses (nitrate leaching, gaseous N emissions); Improve N Use Efficiency (NUE)
Crops: Citrus orchard ('Newhall navel' orange grafted on 'Volkamer' lemon)
Validation: SVM-R cross-validation (R2, RMSE); Internal cluster validity indices (CHI, FSI); V-measure for spatial association between static and dynamic maps
Notes: The study developed a four-step framework for dynamic MZ delineation for SSNM. The SVM-R model successfully estimated canopy N content from multispectral images. The optimal N content range based on the yield envelope curve (October 2019 data) was determined to be between 2.4 and 2.68% N content (%DW). The use of both constant and non-constant variables resulted in dynamic changes in MZ delineation over time. Plots B and C showed a substantial change in MZ spatial pattern when non-constant variables were added, indicating that dynamic delineation is beneficial in these areas. Plot C showed the weakest spatial association between constant-only and constant-plus-N MZs, suggesting that canopy N content strongly influenced the plot's spatial pattern throughout the season.

Title: Scale-dependent geostatistical modelling of crop-soil relationships in view of Precision Agriculture
Country: Iran (Hamedan, Bu-Ali Sina University Research Farm)
Data used for MZ: Multivariate data set including: Soil attributes (Organic carbon (OC), Field capacity (FC), Bulk density (BD), Pre-compression stress, Compression index , Swelling index , Integral root mean surface difference (IRMSD), Clay, Sand); Photosynthetic parameters (Mesophilic conduction (MC), Photosynthetic water-use efficiency (pWUE)); Wheat yield and components (Grain yields (GY), Number seeds per spike (NSPS), Spike height (SH), Plant height (PH), Number of spike (NS))
Auxilary data: Confined compression curve (CCC) parameters (a, b, c, m) derived from Gompertz function fitting; Standardized semivariograms (S0: nugget effect, S1: spherical model with range 545 m, S2: spherical model with range 14692 m)
Data used for validation: Cross-validation metrics for Linear Model of Coregionalization (LMC): Mean Error (ME), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Root Mean Square Standardised Error (RMSSE)
Sensors/DataSources names: LCi device (ADC Bio Scientific Ltd.) (for gas exchange parameters); Uniaxial system or California bearing ratio (CBR) apparatus (for confined compression test); Pressure plate apparatus (for water content); Garmin GPS (implied by georeferencing)
SamplingDensity: 100 locations on a regular grid (150 m x 150 m), with additional samples at 75 m x 75 m and 37 m x 37 m sizes
Resolution: Regular sampling grid size: 150 m x 150 m; Final interpolation grid resolution: 10 m x 10 m
Methods: Multivariate geostatistical approach; Nested linear model of coregionalization (LMC); Factorial cokriging analysis (FCA); Spatial statistical prediction model
Sub-Methods-Internal: Gaussian anamorphosis (for data normalization); Ordinary point cokriging (for interpolation); Regionalized Principal Component Analysis (for scale-dependent factor extraction); Gompertz model fitting (for CCC); Cross-validation
Zones: Scale-dependent field partitions using three retained regionalized factors, each resulting in three homogeneous zones (low, medium, and high classes)
ManagementFocus: Construct a scale-dependent model of spatial soil-crop relationships; Produce field partition at relevant scales; Guide different agronomic operations (e.g., tillage, VRT for irrigation and fertilization/fertigation); Effective site-specific management
Validation: Cross-validation of geostatistical interpolation models (ME, RMSE, RMSSE); Interpretation of factor loadings
Notes: The study utilized a nested linear model of coregionalization and Factorial cokriging to model multivariate correlation structure and calculate regionalized factors at three different scales (nugget effect (S0), short range S1=545 m, and long range S2=14692 m). The longer-scale factor (S2=14692 m, 75% of variance explained) was interpreted as an inverse indicator of **soil compaction** (more related to clay and bulk density) and could guide tillage operations. The first two short-range factors (S1=545 m, 41.87% and 22.57% of variance explained at this scale) were interpreted as **productivity** and **structural properties/photosynthetic activity** indicators, respectively, and could support variable rate technology (VRT) for irrigation and fertilization. The results emphasize the need to include the spatial scale associated with agronomic operations for effective site-specific management.

Title: Delineation and optimization of cotton farmland management zone based on time series of soil-crop properties at landscape scale in south Xinjiang, China
FieldSize: Farmland in southern Xinjiang (81◦17–81◦22E, 40◦28–40◦31 N); The area is characterized by multiple individual fields
Data used for MZ: Time series Sentinel-2 images; Estimated soil-crop characteristics (Soil profile salt content, soil fertility attributes (OM, AP, AK, TP, AN, DOC, and pH), and crop growth conditions characterized by Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) distance of time series NDVI)
Auxilary data: ECa samples (545 samples using EM38-MK2, vertical mode (1.50 m, 0.75 m) and horizontal mode (0.75 m, 0.37 m)); Soil chemical properties (Organic Matters (OM), Available Phosphorus (AP), Available Potassium (AK), Total Phosphorus (TP), Alkaline Hydrolytic Nitrogen (AN), Dissolved Organic Carbon (DOC), and pH values); Soil physical properties (Bulk density, soil water content, and field capacity); Spectral indices (42 indices derived from Sentinel-2, including Salinity indices, Clay index, Gypsum index, Carbonate index, Brightness index, RVI, EVI, NDVI, etc.)
Data used for validation: Accuracy indicators for estimation models: R2, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Lin’s Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC), and Ratio of Performance to Interquartile Distance (RPIQ); Cluster validity indices: Fuzzy Performance Index (FPI), Normalized Classification Entropy (NCE), and Similarity of fuzzy classes (S); Assessment of homogeneity within zones and heterogeneity between zones (using Coefficient of Variance, CV, and mean values); P-values (ANOVA)
Sensors/DataSources names: Sentinel-2 satellite system (MSI); EM38-MK2 electromagnetic conductivity meter (Geonics Ltd.); Built-in global positioning system receiver; LeiCi DDS-307 conductivity meter; SNAP package (Sen2Cor module); R software (Caret package, GWmodel package); MatLab
SamplingDensity: ECa samples: 545 samples (conducted on 1st November 2020); Soil samples: 83 topsoil (0.0–0.2 m) and additional 18 subsoil (0.2–1.0 m) samples; Training/Testing split for salt estimation: 364/181; Training/Testing split for fertility estimation: 59/24
Resolution: Spatial resolution of RS data/estimation: 10 m; Three scales examined for MZ delineation: 10 m, 100 m, and field scales (divided by ridges)
Methods: Time series remote sensing analysis; Machine learning methods; Geographically Weighted Principal Component Analysis (GWPCA); Clustering; Scale optimization
Sub-Methods-Internal: Bootstrap-Random Forest (RF) algorithm (for soil fertility estimation); Temporal Convolution Network (TCN) (for salt content estimation); Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) (for crop growth characterization); Possibilistic Fuzzy C-means algorithm (PFCM) (for clustering); Bi-square kernel function (for GWPCA); Leave-one-out cross-validation (for bandwidth determination); Cross-validation (five-fold and LOOCV); Analysis of variance (ANOVA); Linear regression models (for ECa conversion)
Zones: Three Management Zones (MZ) were finally chosen at the optimal field scale; The optimal number of clusters was 3 at all three scales (10 m, 100 m, and field scales)
ManagementFocus: Delineate and optimize management zones (MZ) in cotton farmland; Implement site-specific management (SSM); Accomplish variable rate input of resources; Address soil salinization and fertility limitations; Provide fertilizer recommendation strategies; Enhance agricultural sustainability and resource utilization efficiency
Validation: Assessment of homogeneity within zones vs heterogeneity between zones (CV); ANOVA (P-values) to test significance of attribute differences between zones; Accuracy assessment (R2, RMSE) of soil property estimation models
Notes: The study estimated soil-crop characteristics with high precision (R2 > 0.65) using time-series Sentinel-2 images and machine learning. The optimal scale for MZ delineation was the **field scale**, which showed superior homogeneity within zones and heterogeneity between zones, and better practicality for agricultural management. The three zones delineated at the field scale were primarily restricted by **salinity and fertility**. The GWPCA algorithm, which accounts for local heterogeneity, showed higher Cumulative Proportion of Total Variance (CPTV) at the field scale (68.15–76.93%) compared to conventional PCA (0.72). Fertilizer strategies included: Zone 1 (high salt, typically fertile): reduce salinity, supplementary phosphate; Zone 2 (moderate): increase organic, potassium, and nitrogen fertilizers, reduce salinization; Zone 3 (poor fertility, low salt): increase nitrogen, phosphorus, and organic fertilizers.

Title: Management zones delineation using fuzzy clustering techniques in grapevines
Data used for MZ: Apparent Electrical Conductivity (ECa); Yield; Soil depth; Elevation; Slope; NDVI (Canopy development, Flowering, Berry set, Veraison, Harvest)
Data used for validation: Grape composition (Sugar content, Total Acidity TA, Sugar/TA ratio)
Sensors/DataSources names: EM-38 (Geonics); Crop Circle ACS-210 active sensor; RTK-GPS (Trimble Ag-GPS 252); DGPS; MZA software; ArcGIS; SPSS
SamplingDensity: Grape quality: 48 grid cells (10 m by 20 m); Soil depth: 48 points; ECa/NDVI: Continuous scanning (4 m swaths); Yield: Whole field harvest (weighed bins)
Methods: Fuzzy c-means clustering
Sub-Methods-Internal: Degree of agreement (Overall accuracy statistic); Fuzziness Performance Index (FPI); Normalized Classification Entropy (NCE); Pearson correlation; Mahalanobis distance
ManagementFocus: Variable rate applications (irrigation, fertilization); Optimizing yield and wine quality; Site-specific vineyard management
Validation: Degree of agreement (overall accuracy statistic) between parameter-based zones and reference zones (Yield and Quality maps)
Notes: Used degree of agreement to select the best combination of parameters for final zoning; High agreement found between yield-based and quality-based zones; Lower yielding zones generally had better grape composition (higher sugar/TA); Early season NDVI and ECa were effective parameters for delineation
Abstract_Snippet: Precision viticulture aims at managing vineyards at a sub-field scale according to the real needs of each part of the field. The current study focused on delineating management zones using fuzzy clustering techniques and developing a simplified approach for the comparison of zone maps. The study was
Notes_decision: All agree (Human + 2 AI models)
Explanations (for each workflows): Fuzzy c-means clustering on multi-variable grids
Methods_Used in detail: Fuzzy c-means clustering (using MZA software); Fuzziness Performance Index (FPI) and Normalized Classification Entropy (NCE) (determination of optimal cluster number); Degree of agreement (overall accuracy statistic used for variable selection prior to final clustering)
Validation Description: Fuzziness performance index (FPI) and normalized classification entropy (NCE) to determine the optimal number of clusters; Overall accuracy statistic evaluating the spatial agreement (pixel-by-pixel comparison) between the algorithmically derived soil/canopy management zone maps and independent reference management zone maps derived from actual yield and grape quality.
